title: What paste-up taught me about church communication
deck: Before anything shipped, it lived on a board — waxed, squared, and marked up in blue pencil. That discipline translates directly to Sunday morning.
body: SAMPLE POST — replace with your own writing. But here's a starter draft in your voice to react against.

Before anything shipped, it lived on a board. Every galley was waxed, placed, squared to the grid, and marked up in non-repro blue before it ever saw a camera. Nothing went out because somebody felt done — it went out because it survived the proof.

Church communication could use more of that. Not the wax — the proof. The discipline of putting the announcement, the sermon series art, the welcome-card copy on the board and asking: does this actually say what we mean, to the person we mean it for?

body.pull: The blue pencil never made it to print. That was the point.

body: The corrections were part of the process, not part of the product. The congregation only ever saw the final pull. That's not hiding the work — that's respecting the reader enough to finish it.

title: Your church's story deserves better than clip art
deck: The gospel is the most compelling story ever told. The bulletin shouldn't look like it lost a fight with a stock-photo library.
body: SAMPLE POST — replace with your own writing.
Marketing creates the story. Communications crafts the story. Production delivers the story. When one of the three phones it in, the whole message limps.
A church doesn't need a bigger budget to communicate with dignity. It needs somebody who cares about the craft on behalf of the message — who believes the medium is part of the hospitality.
